title: Planning a calligraphy project
subtitle: From text choice to final layout: a structured approach
excerpt: A calligraphy piece that works well has been planned before a drop of ink touches the final sheet. Layout decisions made in advance eliminate the most common failures: a title that runs off the edge, a text that is too small for the chosen script, and margins that look accidental. This article walks through the planning steps.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on layout and manuscript planning: advice on choosing paper size relative to text weight, setting margins, and working from a rough layout. Available via Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A working sketch of a calligraphy composition at full or reduced scale, used to check proportions, line breaks, and margin ratios before committing to the final sheet.
The gap between the ascender line of one text line and the descender of the line above it, determining how closely lines sit together.

content.text: Calligraphy planning addresses four questions in order: what is the text, at what scale does it read well, how does it fit on the chosen paper, and where do the margins fall? A
answers all four before the final sheet is touched. Most experienced calligraphers produce two or three rough layouts for every piece; beginners often skip this step and regret it.
Choose the script and nib size

The script determines the visual weight of the text. A Foundational hand at 4 nib-widths x-height reads as medium weight; Italic at 5 nib-widths reads lighter; Gothic Textura at 4 nib-widths reads heavier because of the compressed letterforms. Choose a nib size appropriate to the viewing distance and paper size. A poem on A5 paper might use a Mitchell 4 or 5; a quotation on A2 for framing might use a Mitchell 1 or a Brause 0.
Write the full text on cartridge paper at the intended nib size. Count the number of lines and measure the depth of a single line (baseline to baseline, including
). The total text depth equals lines multiplied by line depth. Measure the width of the longest line. You now have the text block's two critical dimensions.

A classical margin ratio that serves most calligraphy compositions: inner (spine or left) margin 1 unit, head (top) margin 1.5 units, outer (right) margin 2 units, tail (bottom) margin 3 units. On A4 paper, if the inner margin is 20 mm, the head is 30 mm, the outer is 40 mm, and the tail is 60 mm. The resulting text block sits slightly above centre and slightly left of centre on the sheet, which the eye reads as centred because of the way it accommodates the foot of the page as a visual anchor.
Cut and paste to check proportions

Cut the rough-layout text block out and place it on a sheet the size of the intended final paper. Move it around and mark the position where the margins look right. Photograph it or pin it to a board and leave it for an hour. When you return, any proportion that was bothering you will be immediately visible. If the text block looks too large or too small for the sheet, change the nib size and re-test rather than adjusting the margins.

Once the layout is settled, follow the method for the chosen script. For Foundational hand, see the Foundational lower-case and capital tutorials. For Italic, see the Italic minuscule and capital tutorials. For a bordered composition, see the illuminated border design tutorial.
